_meta.author: cowork-seed
_meta.review_notes: Seed example — review pending per blueprint §5.4. Validate against lesson-plan-request and lesson-plan-response schemas.

output.objective: Add two fractions with different denominators by finding a common denominator.

segments.narration: Today we will learn how to add fractions when the bottom numbers are different.
segments.subtitle: Adding Fractions: Different Denominators

visual.title: Adding Fractions
visual.subtitle: Different Denominators
visual.objective_text: Add two fractions using a common denominator.

segments.narration: Picture one half and one third as bars. The pieces are different sizes, so we can't just add the numerators.
segments.subtitle: Different-sized pieces can't be added directly

segments.narration: Rewrite each fraction with six as the denominator. One half becomes three sixths; one third becomes two sixths.
segments.subtitle: Rewrite with a common denominator of 6

segments.narration: Now try two fifths plus one fourth. The common denominator is twenty.
segments.subtitle: Worked example: 2/5 + 1/4

steps.explanation: Rewrite both fractions with 20 as the denominator.

segments.narration: A common mistake is adding the bottom numbers too. Remember the denominators stay the same once they match.
segments.subtitle: Don't add the denominators

mistaken_approach.statement: Students often add both numerators and denominators.

correct_approach.statement: Match the denominators first, then add only the numerators.

visual.why_students_make_it: They apply the rule for multiplying fractions to adding by mistake.

segments.narration: Recap: find a common denominator, rewrite both fractions, then add the numerators only.
segments.subtitle: Common denominator → add numerators

key_points: Rewrite each fraction with that denominator.
Add the numerators — the denominator stays the same.
visual.closing_statement: You can now add fractions with different denominators.

branch_templates.example_question: Why do we use 6 as the common denominator?
branch_templates.branch_narration_template: We pick 6 because it is the lowest common multiple of 2 and 3 — the smallest number both denominators divide into.
A common denominator is a number that both fractions can share as their bottom value. It lets us compare and add equal-sized pieces.
